questions.stem: A subset $U$ of a metric space $(X, d)$ is open when:
options: around each point of $U$ there is an open ball entirely contained in $U$.
$U$ contains all of its limit points.
questions.explanation: Openness means every point is an interior point: for each $x \in U$ there is $r > 0$ with $B(x, r) \subseteq U$. Containing all limit points instead defines a closed set.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A subset $F$ of a metric space is closed when:
questions.explanation: A set is closed exactly when its complement is open. Equivalently, a closed set contains all of its limit points. Boundedness and finiteness are unrelated to closedness in general.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: An arbitrary union of open sets is:
options: neither open nor closed in general.
open only if the union is finite.
questions.explanation: Any union of open sets is open: if a point lies in the union it lies in one of the open sets, which supplies a ball around it inside that set, hence inside the union. This holds for unions of any size.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A finite intersection of open sets is open, but an infinite intersection of open sets:
questions.explanation: Only finite intersections of open sets are guaranteed open. The infinite intersection $\bigcap_n (-1/n, 1/n) = \{0\}$ is a single point, which is not open in $\mathbb{R}$. So infinite intersections of open sets can fail to be open.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: In any metric space $X$, which two sets are always both open and closed?
options: every open ball and its complement.
no sets are both open and closed.
questions.explanation: The empty set and the whole space are always clopen: each is open, and each is the complement of the other, hence also closed. Other clopen sets may exist (in disconnected spaces) but these two always do.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A point $x$ is a limit point (accumulation point) of a set $A$ when:
options: every open ball around $x$ contains a point of $A$ different from $x$.
questions.explanation: A limit point $x$ of $A$ has the property that every neighbourhood meets $A$ in a point other than $x$. The point $x$ itself may or may not belong to $A$; what matters is that $A$ accumulates near $x$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The closure $\overline{A}$ of a set $A$ equals:
options: $A$ together with all its limit points.
questions.explanation: The closure is $\overline{A} = A \cup A'$, where $A'$ is the set of limit points. It is the smallest closed set containing $A$, equivalently the intersection of all closed supersets of $A$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The interior $A^\circ$ of a set $A$ is:
options: the largest open set contained in $A$.
questions.explanation: The interior is the union of all open sets contained in $A$, i.e. the largest open subset of $A$. A point is interior to $A$ when some ball around it lies in $A$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The boundary $\partial A$ of a set $A$ is defined as:
questions.explanation: The boundary is $\partial A = \overline{A} \setminus A^\circ$, the points in the closure but not the interior. Equivalently, every ball around a boundary point meets both $A$ and its complement.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: In $\mathbb{R}$ with the usual metric, the closure of the open interval $(0, 1)$ is:
questions.explanation: The closure adds the limit points $0$ and $1$, giving $\overline{(0,1)} = [0,1]$. Both endpoints are limit points of $(0,1)$ even though neither belongs to the open interval.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: In $\mathbb{R}$, the interior of the closed interval $[0, 1]$ is:
questions.explanation: The interior removes the endpoints, which are not interior points (no ball around $0$ stays inside $[0,1]$). Hence $[0,1]^\circ = (0,1)$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: In $\mathbb{R}$, the boundary of the interval $(0, 1)$ is:
questions.explanation: The boundary is $\overline{(0,1)} \setminus (0,1)^\circ = [0,1] \setminus (0,1) = \{0, 1\}$. Every ball about $0$ or $1$ contains points inside and outside the interval.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A set $D$ is dense in a metric space $X$ when:
questions.explanation: Density means the closure of $D$ is the whole space: $\overline{D} = X$. Equivalently, every nonempty open set meets $D$, so points of $D$ approximate every point of $X$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: In $\mathbb{R}$, the set $\mathbb{Q}$ of rationals has which closure and interior?
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense in $\mathbb{R}$, so $\overline{\mathbb{Q}} = \mathbb{R}$. Its interior is empty because every interval contains irrationals, so no ball lies entirely in $\mathbb{Q}$. Thus $\mathbb{Q}$ is neither open nor closed.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: What is the set of limit points of $A = \{ 1/n : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \}$ in $\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explanation: The only accumulation point is $0$: the terms $1/n$ cluster only at $0$, which is not in $A$. No point $1/n$ is a limit point (each is isolated). So $A' = \{0\}$, and $A$ is not closed.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Every finite subset of a metric space is:
questions.explanation: A finite set has no limit points (around each point one can find a ball excluding the other finitely many points), so it vacuously contains all its limit points and is closed. In particular singletons are closed.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: In $\mathbb{R}$, the set $\mathbb{Z}$ of integers is:
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Z}$ is closed: its complement is a union of open intervals $(n, n+1)$, hence open. It has empty interior since no interval around an integer lies in $\mathbb{Z}$. The integers are a discrete closed set with no limit points.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: An open ball $B(x, r)$ in a metric space is:
questions.explanation: Open balls are open: given $y \in B(x,r)$, the smaller ball $B(y, r - d(x,y))$ lies inside $B(x,r)$ by the triangle inequality. This is exactly why the balls form a basis for the metric topology.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Which statement about interior, closure, and the set itself is always TRUE?
questions.explanation: The interior is contained in $A$, and $A$ is contained in its closure: $A^\circ \subseteq A \subseteq \overline{A}$. Equality of interior and closure happens only for clopen sets, not in general.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A set $A$ is closed if and only if:
questions.explanation: A set equals its closure exactly when it is closed: $\overline{A} = A \Leftrightarrow A$ closed. Dually, $A^\circ = A$ characterizes open sets. Empty boundary characterizes clopen sets, which is stronger.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: In the discrete metric space on a set $X$, which sets are open?
options: only the open balls of radius $1$.
questions.explanation: Under the discrete metric every singleton $\{x\} = B(x, 1/2)$ is open, so every subset (a union of singletons) is open. Hence every subset is also closed: the discrete topology makes all sets clopen.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: A point $x \in A$ that is not a limit point of $A$ is called:
questions.explanation: An isolated point of $A$ is a point of $A$ around which some ball contains no other point of $A$. Every point of a discrete set is isolated; a limit point, by contrast, has nearby points of $A$ in every ball.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Which of the following is a TRUE statement in $\mathbb{R}$?
questions.explanation: The half-line $[0, \infty)$ is closed because its complement $(-\infty, 0)$ is open. The others are false: $(0,1)$ is open not closed, $\mathbb{Z}$ is closed not open, and $\mathbb{Q}$ is neither open nor closed.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: The intersection of an arbitrary family of closed sets is:
options: closed only if the family is finite.
questions.explanation: Arbitrary intersections of closed sets are closed, dual to arbitrary unions of open sets being open (apply De Morgan to complements). Finite unions of closed sets are closed too, but infinite unions of closed sets need not be.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Work in $\mathbb{R}$ with the usual metric and the half-open interval $A = [2, 5)$.
questions.solution: The closure $\overline{A}$ adds the missing endpoint, giving $[2, 5]$; the right endpoint added is the number {{1}}. The interior $A^\circ$ removes the left endpoint, giving the open interval $(2, 5)$. The boundary $\partial A$ consists of the two endpoints $2$ and $5$, so the number of boundary points is {{2}}. Since $A$ does not equal its closure, the set $A$ {{3}} closed.
questions.explanation: $\overline{[2,5)} = [2,5]$ (adds $5$), $[2,5)^\circ = (2,5)$, and $\partial[2,5) = \{2, 5\}$, which has $2$ points. Because $\overline{A} \ne A$, the set is not closed.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Consider the set $A = \{ 1/n : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots \}$ inside $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.solution: Each point $1/n$ is isolated, so the set of limit points of $A$ contains exactly one point, namely the value {{1}}. Because that limit point does not belong to $A$, the set $A$ {{2}} closed. Adding the missing limit point to $A$ gives its closure, so the number of points by which $\overline{A}$ exceeds $A$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The sequence $1/n$ accumulates only at $0$, so $A' = \{0\}$. Since $0 \notin A$, the set is not closed, and $\overline{A} = A \cup \{0\}$ has exactly $1$ more point than $A$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: Examine $\mathbb{Q}$ as a subset of $\mathbb{R}$ with the usual metric, regarding density, closure, and interior.
questions.solution: Every real interval contains a rational, so $\mathbb{Q}$ {{1}} dense in $\mathbb{R}$, and its closure $\overline{\mathbb{Q}}$ is all of $\mathbb{R}$. Every interval also contains an irrational, so the interior of $\mathbb{Q}$ is empty, meaning the number of interior points of $\mathbb{Q}$ is {{2}}. Consequently $\mathbb{Q}$ {{3}} open in $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Q}$ is dense, so $\overline{\mathbb{Q}} = \mathbb{R}$. Its interior is empty ($0$ interior points) because every neighbourhood contains irrationals, so $\mathbb{Q}$ is not open.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis

questions.stem: In $\mathbb{R}$, classify the integers $\mathbb{Z}$ and decide openness/closedness, then count limit points.
questions.solution: The complement of $\mathbb{Z}$ is a union of open intervals, so $\mathbb{Z}$ {{1}} closed in $\mathbb{R}$. Since no interval around an integer lies inside $\mathbb{Z}$, the set $\mathbb{Z}$ {{2}} open. Because the integers are spaced one unit apart, no point is an accumulation point, so the number of limit points of $\mathbb{Z}$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{Z}$ is closed (complement is open) but not open (empty interior). The integers have no limit points because each is isolated, so the count of limit points is $0$.
questions.source: in the style of Rudin, Principles of Mathematical Analysis
